speaker: interviewer
text: Taking last tape mainly about your growing up period. How many years did you spend in the public school, the first public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you remember what year you finished the first public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: What year? [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Not exactly. But I remember there was not-- later on another eight years. It was together about 12 years. And three years nursing school included later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what-- what-- you finished the gymnasium before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: About '34, '35 roughly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in '37, I got married. But then I had those [INAUDIBLE] nursing, three years and though it was right to my marriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you

speaker: interviewee
text: But there was no time to work as a nurse. And '39 was the war started. And I started-- in nursing, I started at the DP camps after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we just go back to the schooling part? You spent-- is it right you spent four years in the gymnasium or six years?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was different than here, entirely different. I think it was about-- all together it was 12 years. It was 6-- less than 6. I think less because I jumped one class. We called is not forms, classes. I jumped one. That was actually five and seven. It was about 12 years together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you would have finished the first school about 1927?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, right to my-- to my marriage. '37, I married.

speaker: interviewer
text: You made mention that in the first school some of the teachers were anti-Semitic.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that some of the teachers were anti-Semitic in the first school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much anti-Semitic.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they--

speaker: interviewee
text: How they showed it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, how did they show it?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know they were privileged children at school sometimes. And there was one teacher-- I knew her name, and I forgot it. I will recall her name. I remember her name. And she was not anti-Semitic. She liked me. Whenever-- whenever you had to do something for school-- I was-- they called me out always because I was a good drawer. Since-- and since three years I used to draw, I-- especially I drew maps. And I loved maps till now. Till now, I remember I had to mark capital cities all over the world with the countries everywhere. And I used to do that. And whenever-- or at chemistry to draw the bottles and everything, whatever is needed, for chemistry, they called me to do it. There was another girl, a Pole. Another teacher said, why do you call always [? Spieglerovna? ?] [? Spieglerovna ?] they called her. She can do it better. She didn't. She couldn't. Often she came up to me, that girl, and ask me how can I do that, show me that. And I didn't want to make enemies. And I did show her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they called out always-- they mis-- they omitted me and called out others. And that I felt it. I just felt it. And other things, there were small things. But once I had to do-- and my brother, elder brother, was very proud of me. How do you say in English huta szklana? Where they blow glasses and all other things, the whole huta szklana from memory. And the teacher said, the teacher would liked me said, [? Spieglerovna ?] he could do-- she could do that. So the other teacher said, no, no, no, there are others. Said, no, let others do it. Let her do it. I did it. And I had to won a small prize. I won a book for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I sat all night at home-- we didn't have a very big home, like here, separate bedrooms-- you know, on the floor all night. We didn't have then electricity in Grodek. Electricity, I remember, came much later. Lwow had electricities, but not Grodek. By lamps, you know those lamps with-- and not inside. You burned it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kerosene lamps.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two lamps on the floor. And my mother said, go to bed, go to bed. You won't be able to get up to school. I didn't. All night, they slept in another room. And I did it in the kitchen on the floor. And I did the whole huta just from memory. And I did it with fires and the colors I knew. I know exactly now the colors, red and orange and yellow. And did the-- and I'm finished it. And it was big like half of that floor. And I brought it in. My teacher was very proud of me. And the other teacher didn't even look at it. She didn't want to see it even. They were anti-Semitic, very much. The schools were very anti-Semitic. And not only this, we had to go on Saturday-- not to school. In school we had Saturday and Sunday off. Sunday for the non-Jews. Saturday for us. Gymnasium didn't allow it. We had to go in Saturday. And my mother didn't mind. But other Jews looked at me like [INAUDIBLE] on Saturday to school. And I did go. They didn't let us off. We had-- Sunday was free, but not Saturday. Pole-- Poland was very anti-Semitic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a majority of Polish children in your first school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where there any Ukrainian children as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But we did in primary school, we did learn Ukrainian language. That's why I know Ukrainian, because especially in eastern Poland there were a lot of Ukraines. That's why Lwow is now Ukrainian. It was Polish, of course. Many more cities are now belong to Russia. They cut it off from Poland, but especially Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your first public school, first primary school, did you have Ukrainian and Polish friends? Or only Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, all mixed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the children--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were no Jewish schools like here, no private schools. There were no private school and no Jewish schools. I think Poland was a poor country, considered even now. They couldn't afford. And privately they were not very wealthy people. They couldn't afford private schools, especially Jewish schools. There was only one public school and one gymnasium. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: In your first public school, did the children live in harmony? Or were there conflicts?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there were no conflicts, otherwise they couldn't go to school like this. No, there were no conflicts. But we-- Jewish children felt it, you know, looks and the Polish had better dress, the children, better looked after, you know, at homes. They had the means. Jews were in that-- in Grodek were rather poor. Some, like ourself, middle class, but a lot of poor people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we get--

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was no help like here that they helped or assisted. Sick people couldn't even afford a doctor because there was no help from any, you know, medical care. So it was a hard life there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we go briefly to a-- a picture of life at home? What did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a merchant. And I think I mentioned to you before that there were three brothers. There were actually five brothers. I don't know them all, the uncles, my father's brothers. And one lived in [? Wojnicz. ?] That was another small township, not far from Lwow, and not far from Grodek. He was wealthy, the eldest brother of my father. He was quite considered then wealthy. And my father and another uncle-- it means another brother of my father-- from another town, small town, used to buy out wheat, like corn and-- no, it's not the same corn, wheat. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] It's wheat altogether. In the-- around the town from Poles who had the farms, the wheat, the fields and everything, buy from them and deliver it to my [? Wojnicz, ?] to the eldest brother. And he used to export it to Czechoslovakia. And that it was like a family concern. But most of it went to my eldest brother who did all the operations. And part of it had-- went to my father and the other uncle. So it was a family concern, merchants. That what I know about.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother looked after the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Housewife, yes. She had so many-- as a matter of fact, she would have about 12 children. But some I didn't know them. They died before I was born, because there were quite a lot of sicknesses after the first World War later on. Elders and elders and like [BACKGROUND NOISE] cholera, and they couldn't treat it as they do it now. And many children died, some at birth, some later. So what I know my mother said she would have had about 12. Six were alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you describe your family as one of being traditionally religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my mother was not very-- although she came from a very religious, orthodox, Hasidic home. My grandparents, I'm named after my grandmother. I never knew any grandparents because my mother was the youngest of the children. And the elder ones, like grandfathers and aunts I never knew. And despite that, she was brought up in a very religious home, like shochtim, [? molim, ?] even rabbis, older ones that I never knew, she was not religious. I think that is because she was a deep thinker. And she liked-- she read a lot. A lot. She read about other religions. And she used to talk with my father, that it makes sense. You know, she doesn't believe-- she used to write about Christianity and to compare the dates. And she said it's not true what they wrote there. And she talked it over with a priest. And often he agreed with her. She was very, very bright.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your mother have her very good Polish from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your mother have such a good Polish from? Where did she have her education?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you that-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] She was not allowed, because when she was young, to attend Polish schools because, first of all, they were Polish, not Jewish. Though her father weren't allow any of his children to go to a Polish school. They didn't attend Polish school. Not a-- not a high school, never thought of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But she wanted to go to school. And her mother knew about it. She didn't tell her husband. She went to school for a few weeks until some people in the city, said to him, to my grandfather, do your know that your daughter, the youngest, goes to a Polish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: He found out where it is, went to school. She had long, blonde hair-- long, blonde hair. She was very beautiful. He grabbed her by the hair and dragged her home all over the city so everybody could see why. And dragged her home by her hair. He nearly killed her because she attended Polish-- a non-Jewish school, a Christian school. And when she came home, somehow, a few days later, she ran quickly to school and told her teacher. And the teacher liked her very much, as I said, because she was really bright. He said, don't worry, you will learn. Said, I want to learn. If I can't learn, I don't want to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the teachers, they made up a plan. They lived in, as I mentioned, a shtetl. That was like a ghetto. Only Jews lived there. I don't remember any non-Jew living there. There were little houses, one near each other. Mostly, they're not-- I don't think that they were flats like here. Everybody had their own house, which passed from grandfather to father and mother, passed on from generations.

speaker: interviewee
text: They lived-- I remember exactly because my mother later showed it to me. On a-- on a little hill there was a house, rather bit bigger. I still remember that house. And there lived my grandfather, grandmother, some aunts, some of their children. And my mother was the youngest. At the back was a door to go out, not the front, but its back. And there at the back, because it was on a hill, you have to go down. I don't know, the called it [? fosa ?], like a valley. And through there, she ran quickly to the school and told the teacher what happened. And the teacher said, expect me, and a few days later we will come up. One or two teachers, we will teach you, to finish even high school. We'll bring books. We'll teach you. So they did. My grandfather never found out, because he was only interested in-- in Hasidic life. He went out from the front, and sit all day or half a day in the synagogues, or whatever or with his sons, shochtin. And they were around, near the synagogue, which was on the other side. He wasn't concerned. He didn't suspect anything. Day by day, they came, the teachers. My mother told me the story. The teachers and taught her, with books, even-- blackboards, they brought. And she wrote everything. She went up to [INAUDIBLE], because she wanted it so much. I still have seen later, after years, years after the war-- I was, I said, in the United States where I found out about my sister. There was living a-- a niece of sister-- of my mother. She showed me the letters of my mother. My mother was in contact with her, used to write her letters in Polish. I came in. I met them there. She said, that's your mother's letters here, I still-- I keep them here. Beautiful. I don't speak such a Polish as my mother did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do they still have the letters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I haven't got even a photo of my mother. I haven't got photos, not of my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you the letters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she wouldn't give it to me. No, she kept it. I don't know if they're still alive. I'm not in contact now. She [INAUDIBLE] used to write there was-- excuse me. There was A Polish paper, a Jewish and Polish [? Shmela. ?] I don't know if anybody remembers it, in the Lwow. My mother used to write articles into the-- not many Jewish women in that time could do that. She used to write. It was in Polish language. Jewish, they wouldn't allow it. I don't remember-- in America, yes. Not in Poland, I don't remember a Jewish paper in Yiddish. I don't remember. But this was-- [? Shmela ?] was a Jewish paper in Polish. That was allowed. And my mother used to write poems and articles. She used to write me a poem-- I haven't got it. That is so many years-- with my name, Spiegler here and over here. It matched up each letter, the words. She was really beautiful. Not one child was like my mother. Very fair with blue eyes, blue eyed. And highly intelligent. But she met a terrible death at-- at Hitler's time.

speaker: interviewer
text: We can go back to that story a little bit later on. Can we now talk about-- quickly-- about home life? You mentioned that your parents, or at least a father was traditionally religious as a Jewish person. What did that mean for what the family did every week? Did you do anything special on Fridays? Did you go--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were not-- the children were not brought up very religious. No, because my mother-- my mother used to-- she didn't want to say it openly, because other people-- I remember only my mother said, when I go to the synagogue, only on the-- only because of neighbors, Jewish neighbors, because they would say she doesn't go the synagogue on Yom Tov.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she used to go on-- on high holidays. And she came home, and she told me-- I don't know why she told me, not others. I was young then, very young. She said, I can't stand it. Those people there-- only women were, you know, with the partition, like it's different here. I don't go to synagogues here either, but sometimes because of the family, my son-in-law and daughters, they go. Though my daughter would go only because of him.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's on the first floor. You know how it's in synagogues here. That was different. It was only ground floor. And it was a partition covered up by-- like-- with a piece of cloth. And behind there were the women. And she told me then, she said, I can't stand it. None of them can read. They have open their machzorim. You know machzorims, books, Jewish. They look into it, but they can't read it. My mother could perfect Yiddish as well. They look at it. And they look away. They talk. But when the men behind the other side-- sometimes it was a glass window. You know when they stand up, when they pray, and they do [? mahila. ?] You know how it is. And lift their voices, then the women start to cry. They couldn't read. They didn't know why.

speaker: interviewer
text: Since they don't know why, my mother said. They can't read. But when they hear the loud voice of the men, then they [WAILING SOUND] start to cry. So she said, how can you stand something like it? Why didn't they learn all right not other languages? My mother knew perfect German as well. They didn't go to school. But Yiddish, they're Jews, why can't they read Yiddish? They couldn't, none of them. So my mother didn't have many friends because of that. She couldn't stand them. She had one friend only, not far, opposite the street where we lived. There was a German family. They were more intelligent people.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had a-- a delicatessen shop. And my mother used to go every evening there and sit with them and talk [BACKGROUND NOISE] with them a little. That was all. Otherwise there weren't-- other Jew did-- not because Jews, but because they were not on her high standards. She had nothing to talk to them about.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that--

speaker: interviewee
text: And she wanted the children to be educated too. But she couldn't send-- first of all in Poland, she couldn't send them all to university, because it was costly, very costly in Poland, not like here. And not only that, Jews were not admitted. Some Jews went to study, as you know, to Italy, some to France. And if you-- finally you got someone who was very bright, you had to pay a lot for it. So that's why not all children studied.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that one of her friends was German. Was it unusual for German people to be in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were of German origin, but for generations, since they would be considered as Poles, Polish nationality, not German.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and going back to how-- the Jewish culture at your home, does that mean that your family didn't keep Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. Yes, we had Shabbat. I don't think Mother did it because she believed so much in it, but it is just a day of rest. And because we had neighbors nearby, we didn't have our own house there. We lived in a-- like flats here, three-roomed flats. And there were others nearby. So, I mean, they communicated. And they knew each other. It was very close. [BACKGROUND NOISE] She had to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother make anything special every Friday? Did she bake bread or make any special food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that she did. But she did it normally for the family. Yes. Prepared even gefilte fish for Saturday.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she baked the bread, special?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she baked by herself. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of bread? What sort of bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: What sort of bread? For Shabbat was a challah. And-- but she didn't bake-- not-- no, we didn't have a bakery. It was only an oven. But nearby was a bakery, a Jewish bakery nearby. So-- and they used to sell bread. So we used to buy bread. And, but challah and other things like cakes, my mother did by herself, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: On Friday, did the whole family sing and pray?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes, it was Fridays evening and Saturday morning. Saturday morning, my father went to the shul, not my mother, and not the children, no one. But I remember on Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur, I don't think my mother fasted. And she didn't tell the children to fast. We did what we wanted. She didn't tell-- she didn't force anyone. And we didn't talk about it. But I know that my father fasted. And my mother said, keep a few pieces of sugar in your pocket. If you feel faint, have a piece of sugar. You're allowed. You don't have to ask a rabbi. She said, I'll tell you, you can do it. I don't think she said that God wanted us to starve. She-- she-- she used to reason and said, he-- God doesn't want to starve her. And anyway he said, if it is a god send or not, we don't know. No one came back to tell us anything. So I don't know to believe or not to believe. And I don't tell my children to believe or don't believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: On Friday nights, did your family sing and pray together at the meal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, at Friday evening only because it was tradition. So we sat Friday evening. My father brought home from the synagogue an 'orach. You know, there were poor people then who had no employment, families. They went-- they traveled around from city to city begging. They were a lot of beggars then, because in Poland, it wasn't like here. They have no work, no profession, most people had no profession because [INAUDIBLE] they didn't learn professions.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a schneider, a-- a-- a tailor. There was a-- a-- how do you call it? A boot maker, repairing boots. And if I recall it now, if a-- if a shoe is torn you throw it out. Then they used to patch it up, and patch it up as long as they could, the poor people. At the shoemaker.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he used to put patches here. And I remember, it's very colorful, the lights there. And-- and people paid him a few grosz and walked until there was another hole. Then they went back. He patched it up again. Not only with shoes, with household goods, pots and pans. There was a blecher. Oh, as I talk, I remember now. As a child, I remember. A blecher means--

speaker: interviewer
text: A tin-- tin maker, tinsmith.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he put patches on-- on pots. If it was a hole, he'd patch it up with a piece of tin and what else. Fire, he used a lamp, a hot lamp. And we paid for it. And I remember exactly who was that blecher, that people went there. And my mother said, if there is a God, he wouldn't allow people to be so poor. He wouldn't allow it. He loved his people, they say, she said. If were are chosen people, he had to do something for us. He doesn't do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. We'll continue on the next tape.
